nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact terms "familial Mediterranean fever" and "autoinflammatory" and clarifies that it is not an infection/autoimmune disease.
Explains treatment using the exact terms "familial Mediterranean fever" and "colchicine", including that colchicine is long-term prophylaxis to reduce attacks and prevent AA amyloidosis.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing colchicine (critical): assesses renal/hepatic impairment, severe blood dyscrasias, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, and concurrent strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors in at-risk patients.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing colchicine (critical): specifically addresses macrolides (e.g., clarithromycin/erythromycin), azole antifungals, protease inhibitors, cyclosporine/tacrolimus, verapamil/diltiazem, and statins/fibrates (myopathy risk).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing (non-critical): asks about prior colchicine hypersensitivity or severe adverse reactions.

Provides safety-net advice: when to seek urgent care (severe abdominal pain with peritonitis signs, persistent high fever, chest pain/shortness of breath, severe diarrhea/vomiting/dehydration, muscle weakness, signs of infection or bleeding).

Addresses monitoring and complications: mentions AA amyloidosis risk and at least one monitoring element (urinalysis/proteinuria or renal function) and/or referral rationale for amyloidosis screening.
Uses shared decision-making and confirms understanding (teach-back or equivalent), including adherence importance and what to do if a dose is missed.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose autosomal dominant FMF, start colchicine with required safety checks, and refer to rheumatology/genetics for confirmation and long-term management.

steps: Explain that the presentation is consistent with familial Mediterranean fever, an autoinflammatory condition with recurrent inflammatory flares.
Discuss first-line long-term prophylaxis with colchicine for familial Mediterranean fever and its goals (reduce flares, prevent AA amyloidosis).

Before prescribing: complete contraindication check (renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy considerations, severe blood dyscrasias) and drug interaction check (strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors; myopathy risk with statins/fibrates), and perform allergy check.
Prescribe oral daily colchicine with plan to start low and titrate based on response and tolerability; counsel on GI effects and toxicity warning signs; outline baseline/periodic labs.

Refer to rheumatology (and/or clinical genetics) for confirmation, genetic counseling/family screening, monitoring for amyloidosis, and escalation to IL-1 inhibitors if colchicine resistant/intolerant.
Create a follow-up plan (timeline, monitoring, and flare management instructions) if discussed in narrative, though not required for the outcome tools.
